nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of clear lagoons and seaward reefs; generally found at depths of 1–73 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on coral polyps, sponges, molluscs, bryozoans, tunicates, crustaceans, and algae in the wild. In captivity, has been known to accept squid, krill, clams, shrimp, and other meaty foods. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: The Arothron Puffer (Arothron meleagris) is a large, bold Indo-Pacific species available in black-spotted, mottled, and golden color phases. Generally considered semi-aggressive, it is best kept singly in a large fish-only system alongside robust tankmates.
careNotes:
The Arothron Puffer occurs across the Indo-Pacific and eastern Pacific and tends to inhabit coral-rich reef and lagoon environments. Three naturally occurring color phases have been described: a dark blue-to-black phase with white spots, a mottled yellow-and-black phase, and an all-yellow (golden) phase. Color-phase changes are rarely observed in captivity. The dark blue-to-black phase is sometimes sold specifically as the "Midnight Arothron Puffer," reflecting its darker overall coloration; it is not a distinct species or morph but simply Arothron meleagris in its darkest naturally occurring color phase.

This species has a distinctive beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that continues to grow throughout life. Hard-shelled foods such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help prevent overgrowth of the teeth. The Arothron Puffer uses its pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins for locomotion, as it lacks pelvic fins entirely.

Like all members of the family Tetraodontidae (the pufferfishes), the Arothron Puffer is capable of inflating its body by rapidly ingesting water when threatened. It should not be allowed to inhale air, as air inflation can be fatal; using a container rather than a net is generally recommended when transferring this species.

Arothron meleagris contains tetrodotoxin, a potent toxin present in the skin and organs that makes the fish highly dangerous to eat. Heavy filtration is generally advised given the species' messy feeding habits. It has been reported to be aggressive toward conspecifics and toward similar-shaped species, and most sources advise keeping it singly. A tank of at least 500 L is the widely cited entry-level minimum for a juvenile or young adult; aquarists are strongly advised to upgrade to the largest system practical as the fish grows toward its 50 cm adult size.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been achieved in captivity. The species is oviparous in the wild. No captive breeding protocols are documented in the available literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific classification, adult size (50 cm), native range, temperature range (24.3–29°C), and diet. Temperature range differs from hobby sources (22.2–25.6°C from LiveAquaria/FreshMarine); averaged across all sources to produce final values of 23–27°C.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), minimum tank size (2000 L), temperament, diet, and native range. ReefApp explicitly states 2000 L is the volume needed for "a medium-size animal kept for several years" — i.e., a long-term adult specimen. This is an aspirational/ideal figure rather than an entry-level minimum. Discrepancy with hobby-source minimums of 473–570 L is resolved in careNotes (2026-06-03).

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), temperature range (24.3–29°C), minimum tank size (3000 L), care level (advanced), and toxicity notes. Tank size of 3000 L is the highest figure gathered; averaged across all 5 sources to produce 1300 L for minVolumeLiters.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), minimum tank size (150 gal / 568 L), temperament (may harass non-robust tankmates), reef safety (not reef safe), and confirmed breeding not accomplished in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet achieved in captivity. Oviparous in the wild.
